name: BYOS (Bring Your Own Signal) Platform Design
definition:
BYOS platform design refers to the architectural shift from multiple independent control loops — each watching its own signal and making its own throttling decisions — to a single unified controller that consumes any number of normalized signals and produces one coherent decision. The acronym (Bring Your Own Signal) is Uber's term; the underlying pattern is broader and applies anywhere multiple controllers risk fighting each other.

The motivating failure mode is split-brain control. When a database has separate rate limiters for inflight concurrency, follower commit lag, write bytes volume, hot partition keys, and memory pressure — each making decisions independently — situations arise where one limiter is opening up admission while another is throttling, or where two limiters simultaneously throttle and over-correct. The system's behavior under stress becomes unpredictable, not because any single controller is wrong, but because they collectively produce incoherent decisions.

The BYOS shift turns this around. A single controller (typically a PID loop) becomes the only decision-maker. Each individual signal is normalized into a contribution to a single 'pressure score' that the controller uses to compute the system's overall response. New signals can be added without architectural change — they just become another normalized input to the same decision loop.

The pattern is more general than its database origin. The same shape appears in: autoscaler design (where independent metrics-based scaling rules can fight), cache eviction (where multiple eviction policies can compete), feature gating (where independent flags can produce contradictory user experiences), and any other system where decisions about a shared resource are made by multiple controllers. The fix is always the same: unify into a single controller consuming normalized inputs.
whenItApplies: Multi-signal control systems where each signal currently has its own controller and the controllers can disagree (load shedders, autoscalers, schedulers)
Building extensible control platforms where new signals will be added over time without re-architecting — the controller becomes the stable interface, signals become the extension point
Diagnosing oscillation or instability in systems with multiple feedback loops — unifying the loops often resolves apparent randomness in behavior

Designing control systems where the policy for combining signals is non-trivial — a unified controller can encode complex prioritization (signal A wins over signal B unless B exceeds threshold X) more cleanly than coordination between independent controllers
tradeoffs: Signal normalization is non-trivial. Each signal has its own units, ranges, and statistical properties. Reducing them to comparable contributions to a single decision requires understanding each signal's behavior under healthy and stressed conditions.

A single controller becomes a single point of failure for the entire control plane. The unified design must be more robust than any of the individual controllers it replaces, because its failure affects all signals simultaneously.

Debugging unified controllers is harder than debugging independent ones. When the system makes an unexpected decision, the root cause could be any input signal — and the interaction between signals matters. Tooling to visualize per-signal contribution to the final decision becomes important.

Pluggable signal architectures have integration overhead. Every new signal must conform to the platform's normalization interface and be tested for stability with the existing signal set. The flexibility comes with required engineering rigor at the integration boundary.
